spots.name: Mito Kairakuen
spots.note: Japan's premier plum destination — 3,000 trees of 100 varieties in one of Japan's 3 great gardens. Annual Mito Plum Festival draws 300,000 visitors.

spots.name: Atami Plum Garden
spots.note: Japan's earliest plum garden — blooms late January thanks to the warm Izu coast. 60 varieties in a scenic valley. Combine with Atami onsen.

spots.name: Kitano Tenmangu
spots.note: Kyoto's most iconic plum shrine — 1,500 trees dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane. Annual Baika-sai (plum blossom festival) in February. Free to view outside; admission to garden.

spots.name: Yushima Tenjin
spots.note: Central Tokyo plum spot — 300 trees and an annual Bunkyo Ume Matsuri. Popular with students before university entrance exams. Free admission.

spots.name: Hanegi Park
spots.note: 400 plum trees of 60+ varieties in Setagaya. Annual Setagaya Plum Festival in mid-February with food stalls and street performers. Free.

spots.name: Dazaifu Tenmangu
spots.note: Kyushu's most celebrated shrine — 6,000 plum trees including the legendary 'tobi-ume' said to have flown from Kyoto to follow its master. Stunning in February.

spots.name: Osaka Castle Plum Grove
spots.note: 1,270 trees of 100 varieties in the castle park — brilliant white and pink blossoms with the castle tower as backdrop. Free to enter the grove.

spots.name: Jonangu Shrine
spots.note: Famous for breathtaking weeping plum (shidarezume) in the shinden-style garden. One of Kyoto's most photogenic spots in winter. Admission required.

spots.name: Odawara Castle
spots.note: 270 plum trees on the castle grounds with samurai-themed plum festival (Feb-Mar). Great stop between Tokyo and Hakone.

spots.name: Hamarikyu Gardens
spots.note: Former imperial villa with a plum grove near Tokyo Bay — elegant Edo-period garden with views of skyscrapers. Admission required.

spots.name: Tsuruma Park
spots.note: Nagoya's favourite plum park — 200 trees in the historic Meiji-era park. The same park hosts cherry blossoms in April. Free.

spots.name: Ikeda Plum Garden
spots.note: 690 plum trees on an urban hillside in northern Osaka — relaxed local atmosphere, annual plum festival in February. Free admission.

spots.name: Tateyama Flower Festival
spots.note: Japan's most famous early nanohana destination — golden fields blanket the Boso Peninsula weeks before cherry blossom. Combines beautifully with Suisen (narcissus) and poppy fields.

spots.name: Minamiaso Valley
spots.note: Sweeping rapeseed fields in the Aso caldera valley with dramatic volcanic peaks as a backdrop. Late March–April coincides with cherry blossom, creating stunning yellow + pink combinations.

spots.name: Nago Nanohana Fields
spots.note: Japan's earliest nanohana — blooms January in subtropical Okinawa when the rest of Japan is frozen. Combines with Nago's early cherry blossom (Kanhi zakura) for a unique winter flower experience.

spots.name: Hitachi Seaside Park
spots.note: Famous for four seasons of flowers — spring nanohana and nemophila (late April) on rolling hills by the Pacific. The same park transforms to cosmos in October and red kochia in autumn.

spots.name: Nobi Hana no Sato
spots.note: Hillside rapeseed fields overlooking Sagami Bay in Yokosuka — easy day trip from Tokyo. February blooms with views towards Miura Peninsula.

spots.name: Awa Yoshino Nanohana
spots.note: Golden nanohana fields along the banks of the Yoshino River, Shikoku's largest river. Scenic cycle routes through the fields are popular in late February.

spots.name: Lake Biwa Nanohana
spots.note: Bright yellow rapeseed fields along the western shore of Lake Biwa in spring. The contrast of yellow flowers against the large blue lake and Hira mountains is stunning.

spots.name: Murakami Nanohana Fields
spots.note: Late-season northern nanohana in Niigata — blooms in April when southern Japan has moved on to cherry blossom. Beautiful along the Japan Sea coast scenery.

spots.name: Ashikaga Flower Park
spots.note: World-famous 150-year-old great wisteria; features purple, white, and double-petal varieties. Admission required.

spots.name: Kawachi Wisteria Garden
spots.note: Stunning hillside wisteria tunnels in Kitakyushu. Open only during bloom season — check dates before visiting.

spots.name: Kameido Tenjin Shrine
spots.note: Classic Edo-era wisteria festival (Fuji Matsuri) over the drum bridge. Free admission.

spots.name: Byodoin Temple
spots.note: UNESCO World Heritage site famous for its wisteria arbor beside the Phoenix Hall. Very photogenic.

spots.name: Kasuga Taisha Shrine
spots.note: Ancient shrine with a famous wisteria in the museum garden. Near Nara deer park. Free to view from outside.

spots.name: Ushijima Shrine
spots.note: Home to the 1,200-year-old Satte Wisteria — one of Japan's oldest. Free to view.

spots.name: Tatebayashi Tsutsujigaoka Park
spots.note: Over 10,000 azalea and wisteria plants. A large park perfect for walking.

spots.name: Fujinomori Shrine
spots.note: Wisteria tunnel in the shrine grounds. Also famous for iris (June). Free admission.

spots.name: Inokashira Park
spots.note: Wisteria grove near the Benzaiten shrine in the park lake. Great combined with cherry blossom walks. Free.

spots.name: Okazaki Park
spots.note: Adjacent to Okazaki Castle — wisteria arbor at the park. Great with the castle backdrop.

spots.name: Motsuji Temple
spots.note: UNESCO World Heritage temple in Hiraizumi with a beautiful wisteria arbor. Later bloom due to northern location.

spots.name: Nodafuji Garden (Osaka)
spots.note: The original 'Noda-fuji' strain of wisteria originates here; Edo-era artists immortalized this spot.

spots.name: Yamato Koriyama Castle
spots.note: Castle ruins with beautiful wisteria gardens. Combined with the famous goldfish town of Koriyama.

spots.name: Horikiri Iris Garden
spots.note: Tokyo's premier iris garden with 6,000 plants of 200 varieties — a classic Edo-period viewing spot along the Keisei line. Free admission. Best early morning to avoid crowds.

spots.name: Suigo-Sawara Iris Festival
spots.nameJa: 水郷佐原あやめまつり
spots.note: Japan's largest iris festival — 1 million flowers in the waterway landscape of Sawara (UNESCO heritage area). Traditional wooden boat rides through the iris fields. Entrance fee applies.

spots.name: Meiji Jingu Iris Garden
spots.note: 150 varieties of iris in the forested inner garden of Meiji Shrine — a serene escape from Harajuku. The late Emperor Meiji himself planted the original iris garden. Admission required for garden.

spots.name: Yato Iris Garden
spots.note: Traditional iris garden in Yokohama with 5,500 irises of 100 varieties on terraced hillsides. Less crowded than Tokyo options — free admission.

spots.name: Motsuji Temple Iris
spots.note: Irises blooming in the ancient paradise garden of Motsuji — a UNESCO World Heritage site in Hiraizumi. Northern Tohoku timing means irises peak in late June–early July. Stunning with the lotus pond.

spots.name: Kamigoryo Shrine
spots.note: Ancient Kyoto shrine with a tranquil iris garden in the inner precincts. Less visited than major Kyoto sights — a peaceful local secret. Irises planted to purify the air in early summer.

spots.name: Izumo Taisha Iris
spots.note: Iris fields near Japan's oldest and most powerful shrine. June visit combines Izumo's spiritual atmosphere with beautiful purple iris blooms. Free to view.

spots.name: Tsuruoka Park Iris
spots.note: 50,000 irises of 100+ varieties in the historic castle grounds of Tsuruoka. Late June–early July in northern Tohoku. Famous annual Tsuruoka Iris Festival with local cultural events.

spots.name: Meigetsu-in Temple
spots.note: The 'Hydrangea Temple' of Kamakura — 2,500 blue hydrangea plants lining the main path. Book early.

spots.name: Hasedera Temple (Kamakura)
spots.note: Clifftop temple with sea views and thousands of hydrangea along the slope path. One of Kamakura's best.

spots.name: Mimurotoji Temple
spots.note: 50 varieties and 10,000 hydrangea plants. Called the 'flower temple' of the Uji area.

spots.name: Engakuji Temple
spots.note: Major Rinzai Zen temple in Kita-Kamakura with hydrangea lining the stone steps. Peaceful atmosphere.

spots.name: Tsurugaoka Hachimangu
spots.note: Kamakura's iconic shrine with hydrangea flanking the Dankazura approach path.

spots.name: Sanzenin Temple
spots.note: Mountain temple in Ohara with mossy gardens bursting with hydrangea. Combine with Jakko-in temple.

spots.name: Hakusan Shrine
spots.note: Central Tokyo shrine hosting the annual Bunkyo Hydrangea Festival (Jun 10-16). 3,000 pots of hydrangea.

spots.name: Fujimori Shrine
spots.note: Serene shrine famous for both iris and hydrangea gardens. June is the best time to visit.

spots.name: Mt. Rokko Alpine Botanical Garden
spots.note: Alpine garden at 865m elevation with a large hydrangea collection. Cooler and later than lowland spots.

spots.name: Naritasan Shinshoji Temple
spots.note: Famous temple near Narita Airport with 10,000 hydrangea in the vast park grounds.

spots.name: Hakone Gora Park
spots.note: French formal garden in the mountains above Hakone with hydrangea bloom alongside mountain views.

spots.name: Yatadera Temple
spots.note: Known as the 'Hydrangea Temple of Yamato' — 60 varieties and 10,000 plants on the hillside grounds.

spots.name: Unzen
spots.note: The entire Unzen resort town is filled with wild hydrangea lining the roads and trails. Very scenic.

spots.name: Takahata Fudoson Temple
spots.note: 7,500 hydrangea plants on the hillside behind the temple. Free to enter. Easy from central Tokyo.

spots.name: Hondoji Temple
spots.note: Called the 'Ajisai Temple' of northern Chiba — 50,000 hydrangea spread across a large temple complex.

spots.name: Farm Tomita
spots.note: The most iconic lavender farm in Japan — the original Hokkaido lavender destination that inspired a generation of visitors. Seven separately themed flower fields including the famous Lavender Field and Rainbow Field. Free entry; July is peak with over 1M annual visitors.

spots.name: Flower Land Kamifurano
spots.nameJa: フラワーランドかみふらの
spots.note: Panoramic flower fields covering the slopes above the Furano Valley — lavender, salvia, marigold and more in sweeping rows. Tractor bus rides across the fields. Great Daisetsuzan views. Admission fee.

spots.name: Lavender East (Farm Tomita)
spots.note: Farm Tomita's satellite lavender field — the largest single lavender field in Japan at 13.3 hectares. Reached by a shuttle from the main farm. Pure lavender, endless purple rows, dramatic mountain backdrop.

spots.name: Kanno Farm
spots.note: Colorful striped flower fields on a hillside in Nakafurano with lavender, sunflowers, and celosia — great photography location. Easy walk from Nakafurano station. Free admission.

spots.name: Hokusei Farm
spots.note: Hillside lavender garden above Nakafurano town — accessible by gondola for panoramic views of lavender fields and Tokachi plain. The view from the top at sunset is unforgettable. Small fee for gondola.

spots.name: Lavender Park Furano
spots.nameJa: ラベンダーパーク富良野
spots.note: In-town lavender park near Furano station — smaller than the main farms but beautifully maintained. Good for those staying in Furano city without transport. Free.

spots.name: Biei Panorama Road Flowers
spots.note: The rolling agricultural landscape of Biei includes lavender, sunflowers, and mixed flower fields along the famous Panorama Road. Best explored by bicycle or rental car. The Ken and Mary Tree and other iconic Biei landmarks are nearby.

spots.name: Nakayama Kogen Lavender
spots.note: Mountain lavender fields in the Nagano highlands — earlier season than Hokkaido (peaks mid-June). Combines well with the Japanese Alps scenery. Less crowded than Furano, good for those in central Japan.

spots.name: Hokuryu Sunflower Village
spots.note: Japan's most famous sunflower destination — 2.3 million sunflowers covering 23 hectares. July–August Sunflower Festival with harvester rides, local food, and endless views. Hokkaido timing means peak in late July.

spots.name: Zama Sunflower Festival
spots.note: 550,000 sunflowers just outside Tokyo — Kanagawa's largest sunflower event, easily reached from Shinjuku in 45 min. Late July festival with food stalls along the flower fields.

spots.name: Akeno Sunflower Fields
spots.nameJa: 明野サンフラワーフェス
spots.note: 500,000 sunflowers in the Hokuto Highlands with stunning Mt. Fuji and Southern Alps views — one of Japan's highest altitude (850m) sunflower fields with clear mountain air. Annual Sunflower Festival with local produce market.

spots.name: Sagawa Sunflower Park
spots.nameJa: さがわひまわりパーク
spots.note: 400,000 sunflowers in rural Kochi, Shikoku — a must for those exploring the Pacific coast. Combines with the beautiful Shimanto River area. Free admission.

spots.name: Moka Sunflower Park
spots.note: 400,000 sunflowers over rolling terrain in northern Tochigi — a popular mid-distance destination from Tokyo. Late July festival with food and craft stalls. Good stop on a Nikko trip itinerary.

spots.name: Higashine Sunflower Festival
spots.note: 300,000 sunflowers in Yamagata with Tohoku mountain backdrop — the northern timing means peak in mid-August when southern Japan fields are winding down. Combined with local Yamagata cherries and peaches.

spots.name: Biei Sunflower Fields
spots.note: Sunflower fields interspersed with the famous lavender and mixed flower fields of Biei's Panorama Road. The combination of sunflowers and lavender in late July is uniquely Hokkaido — visit both in the same day.

spots.name: Aso Sunflower Fields
spots.note: Golden sunflower fields in the Aso caldera with the volcanic peak of Mt. Aso as backdrop — the same fields that show nanohana in spring transform to sunflowers in summer. A year-round floral destination.

spots.name: Showa Kinen Park
spots.note: 4.5 million cosmos flowers in Tokyo's largest national park — a sea of pink, white, and red in October. Different varieties bloom in waves through the month. Easily reached from Tachikawa station. Admission fee.

spots.name: Hitachi Seaside Park (Cosmos)
spots.nameJa: 国営ひたち海浜公園コスモス
spots.note: 4.5 million cosmos plus glowing red kochia (burning bush) in October — a twin display that makes Hitachi one of Japan's top autumn flower destinations. The same park has nemophila in April.

spots.name: Kinchakuda Cosmos
spots.note: 5 million cosmos in a unique horseshoe-shaped meadow formed by the Koma River — one of Japan's top 10 cosmos spots. The natural river setting makes it especially photogenic. Annual festival with food stalls.

spots.name: Asago Art Village Cosmos
spots.nameJa: 朝来アートヴィレッジコスモス
spots.note: Hillside cosmos panorama in rural Hyogo with the Tajima mountains as backdrop — early September peak makes it one of Japan's first cosmos destinations each year. Combine with Takeda Castle ('Cloud Castle').

spots.name: Nara Palace Site Cosmos
spots.note: Cosmos fields surrounding the UNESCO heritage site of the ancient Nara Palace grounds — pink and white flowers against the reconstructed Daigokuden Hall. Free; atmospheric at sunset.

spots.name: Makomanai Cosmos Park
spots.note: Hokkaido's most famous cosmos park near Sapporo — early September peak (northern timing). Combines with autumn koyo which starts earlier in Hokkaido. Free admission.

spots.name: Akagi Natural Park Cosmos
spots.note: Hillside cosmos with early autumn leaf color — at 550m altitude the cosmos peak with the first hints of koyo on the slopes of Mt. Akagi. Combined with the park's many other seasonal flowers.

spots.name: Azumino Cosmos Road
spots.nameJa: 安曇野コスモスロード
spots.note: Cosmos lining the rural cycling roads of Azumino with the Northern Alps as a dramatic backdrop — Japan's most scenic cosmos cycling course. Late September–October combines cosmos with early mountain snow on the peaks.
